title-group.article-title: Multilevel PFC Converters for Efficient EV Battery Chargers

institution-wrap.institution: Electrical and Electronics Engineering, VNIT Nagpur
Electrical and Electronics Engineering, BVRITH College of Engineering for Women
Electrical and Electronics Engineering, Chhatrapati Shivaji Institute of Technology

abstract.p: Two-stage electric vehicle (EV) charging architectures typically include a rectification stage followed by a DC-DC conversion stage. Multilevel rectifiers (MLRs) are emerging as superior options for the rectification stage in EV chargers due to their lower voltage stress on switches, improved harmonic performance on the grid side, reduced dv/dt stress, and higher voltage handling capability. In this paper, a five-level rectifier combined with a phase-shift full-bridge (PSFB) converter is implemented for EV charging applications. The rectifier inherently balances capacitor voltages and maintains unity power factor (UPF) at the source side under various operating conditions. To validate the system's suitability for real-time EV charging, a comprehensive performance analysis is conducted, covering loss and efficiency calculations. The EV battery charger has been mathematically modelled, simulated in MATLAB and evaluated using real time validation.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: EV sales are projected to grow significantly in the coming years, offering a cost-effective alternative to conventional internal combustion engine ICE vehicles. EVs are more economical in terms of operational costs, leading to substantial savings [1]. However, the widespread integration of EVs into the power grid raises concerns regarding grid reliability and power quality due to increased peak demand [2], [3]. On-board chargers (OBCs), commonly integrated with EVs, can serve as a potential solution to mitigate these issues and improve grid power quality [4]. OBCs are well-suited to alleviate range anxiety for users, as they enable vehicles to recharge from any accessible charging outlet. The commonly adopted architecture for on-board charging systems consists of a two-stage power conversion process: an AC-DC front-end rectifier followed by an isolated DC-DC converter. The primary stage is responsible for maintaining a UPF at the input and supplying a stable DC link voltage to the secondary stage. To comply with utility grid standards regarding efficiency and power factor, PFC is essential at the AC-DC conversion stage. This is typically achieved using PFC rectifiers, which integrate a full-bridge diode rectifier and a DC-DC boost converter to improve power quality [5]. These chargers convert the AC supply from the grid into a regulated DC output suitable for charging the EV battery. With advanced topologies and control strategies, onboard chargers can also perform additional functions such as voltage regulation, harmonic mitigation, and power factor correction [6]–[8].

In recent years, multi-level rectifiers (MLRs) have seen widespread adoption for power factor correction (PFC) due to their numerous advantages, including a modular structure, higher operating voltage capability, reduced switching losses, and improved converter efficiency-surpassing traditional diode bridge and two-level rectifiers [9]–[11]. Comparisons show that MLRs significantly reduce current distortion and terminal voltage fluctuations compared to conventional solutions. Despite ongoing improvements in MLR performance, semiconductor devices remain the most vulnerable components in the system, posing a higher risk of failure and thus limiting overall system reliability [12].

In this work, a five-level rectifier with six active switches, along with a PSFB converter, has been implemented to develop a multilevel PFC-based EV battery charger. The key contributions include a detailed analysis of the operation and control of the five-level rectifier, incorporating a novel switching scheme. Additionally, a control strategy is designed for the PSFB DC-DC converter to support a wide output voltage range suitable for battery charging. The author's contributions in the proposed work encompass a comprehensive analysis of the operation and control of a PFC five-level rectifier incorporating a novel switching strategy. Additionally, a control scheme is developed for a PSFB DC-DC converter to enable a wide output voltage range suitable for battery charging. The key challenges addressed include achieving unity power factor at the input, ensuring capacitor voltage balancing in the five-level rectifier, maintaining a regulated DC output voltage, and efficiency of the battery charging system. The specific contributions of the proposed work are summarized as follows:
1.
Design and implementation of a PFC five-level rectifier that ensures unity power factor at the input and delivers a stable DC-link voltage. 2.
Leveraging the advantages of the multilevel rectifier to minimize voltage stress on power switches, thereby enhancing system reliability. 3.
Introduction of a switching technique that enables complementary switching, which reduces the requirement for gate driver circuitry. 4.
Development of a decoupled control strategy for the power converters to effectively regulate various system parameters.

The paper is structured as follows: Section 2 outlines the proposed system's description. The closed-loop control for each sub-converter is described in Section 3. Section 4 presents the loss calculation and efficiency for various components of the system. Section 5 discusses real-time validation in various operating modes, and Section 6 presents the conclusion.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: Multilevel Converter Based Charger
sec.p:
Figure 1 illustrates the schematic of a two-stage EV battery charger. This charger integrates ten power switches and four diodes (labelled S1-S6, S7-S10, and D1-D4), along with a line inductor (L) and a high-frequency transformer (HFT). The system's converters function independently under a decoupled control strategy [13]. A key component, the five-level rectifier, plays an essential role in battery charging and grid interfacing. The design enhances converter efficiency by minimizing voltage and current stresses on the switching devices. Gate signals for the PFC rectifier switches are generated using a PWM approach. The PSFB converter, equipped with a high-frequency transformer, provides galvanic isolation between the grid and the load. It transfers energy from the DC-link capacitor to the load via phase-shifting. A PI controller manages the output current, ensuring quick response, stable dynamics, and low steady-state error. The system uses a reference signal to regulate the DC output voltage and maintain consistent load current. Figure 2 presents the applied switching strategy over a full 20 milliseconds cycle. Five distinct voltage levels are generated using appropriately timed PWM pulses sent to the switches. These pulses are derived by comparing a reference sine wave with four triangular carrier signals. The resulting switching signals are then assigned to the corresponding switches responsible for generating and combining the required voltage levels. As a result, a group of PWM pulses are combined to generate the switching signals for specific switches.
Fig. 1:
Circuit diagram of ev charger
Fig. 2:
Gate pulses for corresponding switches

Figure 3 illustrates how power flows from the input to the output in the PSFB converter. The input voltage $V_{d c}$ on the primary side is phase-shifted relative to the output voltage $V_{O}$ on the secondary side by an angle $\phi$. The primary current lags behind both the output voltage by an angle $\theta$ and the primary voltage by an angle $\beta$, with the relationship between these angles defined as $\theta=\beta-\phi$. During the power transfer process, current flows through the path $S 7 \rightarrow$ transformer primary winding → $S_{10}$. This transfer phase concludes when switches $S_{7}$ and $S_{10}$ are turned off, controlled by a specific PWM signal. The amount of power delivered from the primary to the secondary side depends on the phase shift between the two legs of the full-bridge circuit. Each active switch operates with a 50 % duty cycle, and the maximum allowed phase shift between the legs is 180 degrees. When switches $S_{8}$ and $S_{9}$ are activated, the current path changes to $S_{8} \rightarrow$ transformer primary $\rightarrow S_{9}$, beginning the next power transfer cycle.
Fig. 3:
Waveform for psfb converter
Fig. 4:
Control technique for five-level rectifier

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Control Design
sec.p: Here, d represents the duty cycle of the PSFB converter. To minimize variable error, the control strategy incorporates an anti-windup limiter. The PSFB converter's current controller is developed and validated in conjunction with a five-level rectifier. To ensure the system meets performance criteria, parameter tuning is carried out following the integration of both voltage and current control loops.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Loss Calculation
sec.p: Evaluating power loss is crucial for assessing a system's conversion efficiency. Since power losses manifest as heat within the circuit, minimizing them is vital for effective thermal management. Semiconductor power losses generally fall into three categories: blocking, switching, and conduction losses. Among these, blocking losses are typically negligible due to the minimal current flow in this mode. Consequently, switching and conduction losses are the primary sources of power dissipation in semiconductors. The following section outlines the loss calculations for each component individually:

Therefore, the above formulas for calculating component power losses enable a comprehensive analysis of system losses. Figure 6 illustrates the loss distribution across each element. For the proposed $1 k W$ system, the total power loss is $43.18 w a t t s$, resulting in a maximum efficiency of 95.86 %.

sec.label: V.
sec.title: Results and Discussion
sec.p: The charging system has been tested at an operating power of up to $1 k W$. It operates with an input supply of 230 V AC at $50 H z$, and the corresponding waveforms are presented in this section. The performance of the charger is validated through results from the AC-DC conversion stage, DC-DC conversion stage, and the complete charging system. Figure 8 presents the steady-state results for the rectifier. It converts a 325 V peak AC input into 400 V DC, maintaining a stable DC link voltage as shown in Figure 7(a). The source current remains sinusoidal and in phase with the grid voltage, confirming a unity power factor at the input. A five-level voltage waveform at the rectifier side is also illustrated. Figure 7(b) displays the DC link output voltage and current, while Figure 7(c) highlights the voltage stress across each power device. Figure 7(d) shows the current flowing through the power switches. These observations confirm that both the voltage stress and current remain within safe operating limits, ensuring reliable operation of the battery charger.

Figure 8 presents the steady-state results of the PSFB DC-DC converter, where the input is the 400 V DC link voltage, as shown in Figure 8(a). The corresponding output voltage and current waveforms are displayed in Figure 8(b). These waveforms indicate that the converter delivers regulated output values suitable for battery charging applications. The system has been primarily tested under full-load conditions.

sec.label: VI.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p:
This paper presents the implementation of a two-stage EV battery charger comprising a five-level rectifier and a PSFB converter. The system maintains high power quality by achieving unity power factor. The five-level rectifier reduces the stress over the switches and synthesizes the input voltage waveform to maintain the power factor of overall charging system. The proposed switching scheme ensures balanced capacitor voltage and unity power factor, eliminating the need for additional control circuitry and sensors. Additionally, the implemented control strategy for the PSFB converter maintains a stable output voltage under steady-state conditions. Component-level power loss analysis has been performed to evaluate the overall system efficiency. Moreover, the presented EV battery charger performs well over the desired operating range in terms of improved power quality, reduced stress and enhanced efficiency.
Fig. 7:
Results depicting (a) rectifier input voltage and current with five-level voltage. (b) dc link voltage and current. (c) & (d) voltage and current across the rectifier switches.
Fig. 8:
Results depicting (a) input dc link voltage and current. (b) output voltage and current
